coredata.dc:title: Lanthanum mediated rutin yellow-fluorescent carbon dots as multifaceted sensing probes for the detection of calcium ions in melanoma and plant cells
coredata.dc:creator: Lakshmi, Buddolla Anantha
Sangubotla, Roopkumar
Kim, Jongsung
Ha, Hyung-Tae
Kim, Sanghyo
coredata.dc:description: Calcium (Ca2 +) as a signaling ion and intracellular second messenger plays a crucial role in living organisms for various cellular functions. In the present work, we have designed a novel yellow-fluorescent carbon dots (LERCDs) using lanthanum, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), and rutin as precursors for the sensing of Ca2 + ions. In this sense, a combination of hydrothermal and reflux methods was adopted. The as-designed LERCDs display bright yellow color emission in the aqueous solutions with a high quantum yield (23.8%). The LERCDs showed excitation-independent emission property along with magnificent photostability and time stability. The LERCDs show potential fluorescence quenching response towards the Ca2+ ions in the concentration range of 0–25 μM with a detection limit of 2.19 μM. The LERCDs have studied for extracellular sensing of Ca2 + ions in both melanoma cell lines (A375) and onion epidermal cells by employing fluorescence microscopy. The LERCDs facilitate low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility features in A375 cells. The practicality of LERCDs was studied in biological samples like the human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Yellow-fluorescent
Hydrothermal and reflux method
Multifaceted sensing probe
Ca2+ ion detection
Extracellular sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extracellular sensing of Ca2+ using LERCDs in A375 cells
Extracellular sensing of Ca2+ in plant cells
Extracellular sensing of the Ca2+ ions using A375 cells
Extracellular sensing of the Ca2+ ions using plant cells

Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the R&D Program from the Society of the National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning ( 2015M3A9E2031372 ) and by the Gachon University research fund of 2019 ( GCU-2019-0385 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A and B) TEM images of the LERCDs (inset shows the HRTEM image of the LERCDs). (C) Gaussian particle size distribution for the LERCDs, and (D) XRD pattern of the LERCDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra for the LERCDs (black line) and Ca2+-treated LERCDs (red line). (B) PL excitation (black curve) and emission spectra (red curve) of the LERCDs, inset shows the images of the LERCDs under daylight (left) and UV light (right). (C) excitation-independent emission spectra for the LERCDs from excitation wavelengths 350–450 nm, and (D) Fluorescence quenching behavior of LERCDs in PBS (pH: 7.4, 1× concentration). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A) FT-IR spectra of (i) LERCDs and (ii) Ca2+-treated LERCDs. (B) Zeta potential of the LERCDs before and after treatment with Ca2+ (10 and 70 μM).

(A) XPS survey spectra of (i) LERCDs (ii) Ca2+-treated LERCDs, (B-G) high-resolution XPS spectra of Cl 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, O1s, Na 1 s, and La 3d from the LERCDs and (H) high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ca 2p from the Ca2+-treated LERCDs.
(A) PL emission behavior of the LERCDs using various Ca2+ ion concentrations from top to bottom: 0–70 μM. (B) Linear relationship between (F0-F/F0) and Ca2+ ion concentration.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism underlying LERCDs PL sensing of Ca2+ ions.

(A) Selectivity of the LERCDs in the presence of various metal ions in addition to Ca2+. The concentrations of the Ca2+ ions were 70 μM and the other metal ions was 100 μM, respectively. (B) Shows the photographs (obtained using a 365 nm UV light) of the PL intensity of all of interferences (i-xiii).

Extracellular sensing of the Ca2+ ions in melanoma (A375) cells using the LERCDs. (A) shows the LERCDs with A375 cells without Ca2+. The LERCDs after the addition of different concentrations of the Ca2+ ions (B) 10 μM, (C) 40 μM, and (D) 70 μM in A375 cells.

Extracellular sensing of the Ca2+ ions in onion epidermal cells using the LERCDs. (A) shows the LERCDs in onion epidermal cells without Ca2+ ions. The LERCDs with different concentrations of the Ca2+ ions (B) 10 μM, (C) 40 μM, and (D) 70 μM in onion epidermal cells.
Schematic diagram describing the synthesis of the LERCDs using hydrothermal treatment and reflux reactions.
Comparison of the LERCDs with other fluorescent CDs for the sensing of Ca2+.

77 pM (in human serum)
92 pM
(in DI water)
Citric acid, ethylene diamine, and EGTA
Intracellular sensing of Ca2+ (live cells) and Fluorescence detection of Ca2+

Intracellular imaging and
Fluorescence sensing of Ca2+

Extracellular sensing of Ca2+ ions (live cells and plant cells) and
Fluorescence sensing of Ca2+ ions

head.ce:title: Lanthanum mediated rutin yellow-fluorescent carbon dots as multifaceted sensing probes for the detection of calcium ions in melanoma and plant cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Seongnam 461-701, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Seongnam, 461-701, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Gachon University, 1342 Seongnam Daero, Seongnam-Si, Gyeonggi-Do 13120, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this wok.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Calcium (Ca2 +) as a signaling ion and intracellular second messenger plays a crucial role in living organisms for various cellular functions. In the present work, we have designed a novel yellow-fluorescent carbon dots (LERCDs) using lanthanum, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), and rutin as precursors for the sensing of Ca2 + ions. In this sense, a combination of hydrothermal and reflux methods was adopted. The as-designed LERCDs display bright yellow color emission in the aqueous solutions with a high quantum yield (23.8%). The LERCDs showed excitation-independent emission property along with magnificent photostability and time stability. The LERCDs show potential fluorescence quenching response towards the Ca2+ ions in the concentration range of 0–25 μM with a detection limit of 2.19 μM. The LERCDs have studied for extracellular sensing of Ca2 + ions in both melanoma cell lines (A375) and onion epidermal cells by employing fluorescence microscopy. The LERCDs facilitate low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility features in A375 cells. The practicality of LERCDs was studied in biological samples like the human serum.

ce:section.ce:para: Calcium ions (Ca2+) mainly account for physiological and biochemical functions including the coagulation of blood, cell/neural signaling, and skeletal growth. Also, Ca2+ as a second messenger controls differentiation, proliferation, migration, and apoptosis, etc. [1]. Abnormal Ca2+ levels in cells likely responsible for neurodegenerative disorders like Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), and Huntington's disease (HD) [2–4]. Again, evidence suggests that dysregulation of Ca2+ levels in cancer cells is related to tumorigenesis and metastasis [5]. Thus, sensing Ca2+ ions at the cellular level is gaining the center of attention for current researchers.

Currently, several methods are accessible for the detection of Ca2+ ions, including atomic absorption spectrometry [6], the calorimetric method [7], inductively coupled plasma [8], ion-selective electrode method [9], and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) [10]. Unfortunately, the high economic burden, experienced personnel, and time-consuming procedures are limiting these methods. Comparatively, fluorescence-based methods are highly demanding because of interesting features such as sensitivity, selectivity, economical, and rapid analysis time.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a kind of emerging zero-dimensional nanomaterial with a size of less than 10 nm [11]. The CDs are highly striking in the current research area owing to the fascinating advantages such as low toxicity, high biocompatibility [12–14], hydrophilicity [15], and superior thermal stability [16,17] when compared to fluorescent dyes [18] and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [19]. Especially, these advantages making them ideal nanomaterials for biosensing [20–22], bio-imaging [23,24], and drug delivery [25,26] applications.

Kim and Wang's groups have developed CDs using EDTA and peptide for detecting Ca2+ in serum, respectively [27,28]. Unfortunately, these studies did not investigate Ca2+ detection in either in vitro or in vivo models. In the beginning, the Sreenivasan group has modified CDs using biocompatible hyaluronic acid (HA) and glutamic acid (GA) and used for the detection of Ca2+ ions in bone [29]. But low wavelength emission (i.e. blue- color fluorescence) has highly limited their sensing scope in other biological tissues. Yue et al. designed ethylenebis-(oxyethylenenitrilo) tetraacetic acid (EGTA) modified CDs via the secondary hydrothermal method and employed for intracellular sensing of Ca2+ ions [30]. But, long duration of synthesis and blue fluorescence further require betterments for deeper investigations. D. Chen group designed the capsicum-derived green color-emissive CDs and by utilizing ARS (alizarin red S), imaging of intracellular Ca2+ ions was achieved [31]. The application of the dye (ARS) is limiting their application in the in vivo models.

Till now, limited research has reported the rare earth element-derived CDs. For example, He et al. designed the gadolinium-doped CDs for bio-imaging modalities like magnetic resonance and fluorescence [32]. Though lanthanum (La3+) is highly popular as a valuable precursor and only a few reports are available for the synthesis of CDs [33–35]. Noteworthy, the incorporation of La3+ for designing CDs has become advantageous due to the distinctive atomic structures, photoluminescence (PL) properties, and heteroatom doping. Unfortunately, blue shifting of fluorescence was observed in the case of La3+-incorporated CDs due to the interaction between La3+ and surface oxygen-containing groups and the quantum size effect [33]. In this case, the PL tuning approaches can overcome this issue while in the CDs preparation. Yang et al. developed La3+ doped CDs and tuned the fluorescence from blue to green [35].

Flavonoids with anti-inflammatory, anti-cancer, and antioxidant properties are acting as the convenient precursors in nanomaterial synthesis. In this context, rutin is widely employed as a bioactive agent for the conjugation or surface modification of CDs [36]. Recently, rutin becoming a potential bioflavonoid in the construction of various nanomaterials due to its polyphenolic and potential reducing capabilities. For instance, Li et al. designed a CDs/rutin system which is a dual-mode sensor (i.e. colorimetric and fluorimetric) for sensing of aluminum (Al3+) metal ions. In this system, rutin acts as the selective recognition group for Al3+ by forming a yellow complex [37]. Moreover, rutin facilitates both antioxidant and chelating properties in transition metal ions such as Al3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, and Zn2+. In this manner, rutin forming metal-flavonoid complexes and eliminating the excessive free radicals. Thereby, rutin can also be efficiently utilized in the detection of various metal ions [38]. F. G. Menezes group has developed the rutin-modified silver nanoparticles for the detection of Fe3+ ions. Again, the introduction of flavonoid skeleton in the CDs provide not only biocompatibility also the stability and strong reducing abilities to the CDs [39].

In general, the CDs are mostly displayed in the blue and green-color emission, which have a low range of wavelengths. These CDs have been restricted for the sensing of the deeper biological tissues. Significantly, extreme autofluorescence is the major concern that is showed by the biological tissues. In this regard, long-wavelength emission-based CDs (i.e. yellow and red light) with high QY are beneficial to solve this problem [40–45]. Liu et al. engineered yellow-emissive manganese (Mn2+) doped CDs with high QY (i.e. 68.6%), which attributed to the Mn2+ ion doping. It facilitates the polymerization and enhanced conjugated sp2-domains and surface states on the CDs [44]. Cheng et al. developed zinc (Zn2+) ion-doped CDs with strong yellow emission and high QY (i.e. 51.2%). The surface passivation of CDs with Zn2+ ions leads to high QY [45]. In addition, lanthanum chloride (LaCl3) as a broadly renowned Ca2+ channel blocker, has been used for studying the electrophysiological experiments in plants [46]. Therefore, it strikes the use of LaCl3 in the present work to study the detection of Ca2+ ions. We have selected A375 and onion epidermal cells as models for animal and plant cells, respectively. These cells provide the basis for the LERCDs in the Ca2+ ion detection. In this work, novel yellow emissive LERCDs were synthesized from LaCl3, EDTA, and rutin by combined hydrothermal and reflux methods. Interestingly, these LERCDs are highly promising with long-wavelength emission, excitation wavelength-independent emission, excellent biocompatibility, and maximum photostability. Finally, the LERCDs were successfully employed as a multifarious probe for the detection of the Ca2+ ions in the aqueous solutions, cancer cells, plant cells, and human serum due to these outstanding properties.

Lanthanum (III) chloride hydrate (LaCl3·xH2O), rutin hydrate, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), calcium chloride (CaCl2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), copper (II) chloride (CuCl2), mercury nitrate monohydrate (Hg (NO3)2·H2O), cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate, iron (II) chloride (FeCl2), iron (III) chloride (FeCl3), aluminum (III) chloride (AlCl3), chromium (III) chloride, quinine sulfate, and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. The MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay kit was purchased from Bio Vision Milpitas, USA. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin-streptomycin and trypsin-EDTA were purchased from Gibco Laboratories, South Korea. Cell culture labware was obtained from NEST Scientific, USA. DI water was used for all the experiments and all chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the LERCDs were processed on a Tecnai G2 F20S-TWIN 200 kV electron microscope. A scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4700, HITACHI, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 20 kV was employed to study the elemental constitution of the LERCDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the LERCDs were investigated using Rigaku ultima III X-ray diffractometer, in continuous scanning 2θ mode with a scan speed of 3°/min and 40 kV and 30 mA equipped with Cu sealed tubes (λ = 1.54178 Å). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the LERCDs before and after the treatment of Ca2+ were measured using a Bruker Vertex high-resolution 70 FT-IR spectrometer. Total survey and individual high-resolution XPS spectra were analyzed using the Thermo Scientific K-Alpha™ X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (100–4000 eV range of motion) a 180° double-focusing hemispherical analyzer, and 128-channel detector along with an Al Kα micro-focused X-ray source. Zeta potential (ζ) of the LERCDs before and after adding of Ca2+ was measured with the Zeta PALS instrument (Brookhaven Instrument Corp., USA). The absorption spectra of the LERCDs were recorded using a Varian Cary 100 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. All Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were obtained from a fluorescence spectrometer (Photon Technology International (PTI), Quanta Master NJ, USA) arranged with a Xe lamp (Arc Lamp Housing A-1010B™), power supply (Brytexbox) and monochromator. The pH of the solutions was measured using a Thermo Scientific pH meter. Thermal stability investigations were conducted using an SDT Q600 (Version: 20.9 and Build: 20) (TGA-DTA) analyzer under N2 conditions, a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 and the flow rate of around 100 mL min−1. The quantum yield of the LERCDs was determined by the Fluoro-Q2100 quantum yield system (at excitation wavelength at 431 nm) using quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a standard. The quantum yield was calculated based on the following equation:
(1)
Quantum Yield = Ec /
La − Lc

Here, Ec, La, and Lc are all included in the emissions developed from the direct excitation light, total amount of excitation light and the amount of light calculated after direct excitation light, respectively [47]. The Fluorescence lifetime decay experiment was carried out using a Photon Technology International, EasyLife II fluorometer (auto-adjustable excitation wavelengths from 260 to 650 nm). EasyLife II software was employed using a serial RS232 and USB connection to obtain the curve fitting data. The instrument response function (IRF) was achieved as a background signal employing a 1% LUDOX® LS, colloidal silica suspension in the water. The extracellular sensing of Ca2+ using LERCDs in A375 cells was evaluated using fluorescence microscopy.

The LERCDs were prepared via a combination of hydrothermal treatment and reflux reaction. Initially, LaCl3·xH2O (1.5 g) and EDTA (1 g) were dissolved in 50 mL mixture of ethanol and water (1:1) and stirred for 30 min. The reaction mixture was sealed in a 75-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After the completion of the hydrothermal reaction, the brown solution was cooled to room temperature. This solution was transferred to a round bottomed flask (100-mL) for the reflux reaction. Immediately, the pH of the solution was adjusted to >9 by the dropwise addition of NaOH (0.1 M). Meanwhile, rutin hydrate (30 mg) was added to the solution and refluxed at 50 °C for 2 h. The brown colored solution was changed into an orange-yellow colored gel-like solution (Fig. S1) indicating the formation of LERCDs as illustrated in Scheme 1 . To purify the resulting LERCDs, the solution was centrifuged (9000 rpm, 10 min) and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da, molecular weight cut-off) against DI water for 24 h. These, LERCDs solutions were dried to powder using vacuum drying and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Healthy human serum was chosen as the test sample as it comprises low levels of Ca2+ ions. For this, we followed the previous method with little modifications [27]. Human serum and nitric acid (0.1%) were properly mixed in 1:2 proportions and immediately ultrasonicated about 15 min. This mixture was heated at 180 °C for 4 h and then the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 7.2. After that, the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da, molecular weight cut-off) for 24 h to release the bound Ca2+ ions present in the serum. Through this approach, Ca2+ ions that are bound to the proteins like albumin and other minerals like anions exist in human serum were efficiently released. Hence, remained free Ca2+ ions that are an active form can be thoroughly detected by the LERCDs in human serum.

Solutions comprising 100 μL of Ca2+ and 500 μL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were added to a cuvette and then 100 μL of the LERCDs (0.15 mg L−1) were added and the solution was made up to 2 mL with DI water. Immediately after the 30 min incubation, PL spectra were recorded for each sample using an excitation wavelength of 431 nm. In addition, fluorescence sensing experiments were carried out using different concentrations of Ca2+ (0–70 μM). All PL emission intensities of the samples were recorded at 567 nm.

Human melanoma cell lines (A375) were grown in Dulbecco's Minimum Essential Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin. The cultured cells were incubated at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. After reaching 80% confluency the cells were sub-cultured.

A375 cells were grown in 96-well plate with complete medium and were incubated for 24 h at 5% CO2 and 37 °C. After incubation, the medium was discarded, and different concentrations (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 and 120 μg mL−1) of cisplatin and LERCDs were added to the cells and incubated for a further 24 h. Upon incubation, the medium was removed and MTT reagent was added for another 3 h. Finally, the MTT solvent was added and incubated for 30 min at room temperature with slight shaking and absorbance was recorded at 570 nm using a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extracellular sensing of Ca2+ using LERCDs in A375 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Using six-well plates and coverslips, cells were cultured in the correct medium and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. After incubation, the medium was removed and the cells were washed with Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS) solution to remove the residual medium. For the control LERCDs (50 μg mL−1) were added to the cells. Different concentrations of Ca2+ ions (0, 10, 40, and 70 μM) were mixed with LERCDs and added to the cells. These were incubated at 37 °C for 1 h in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. The coverslips were removed and rinsed with DPBS and the cells were imaged on a fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extracellular sensing of Ca2+ in plant cells
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the fluorescence sensing potential of the LERCDs, epidermal cells of onion were used as a model system. The epidermal layer of a fresh onion was peeled off and soaked in LERCDs solutions and LERCDs containing Ca2+ at different concentrations (10, 40, and 70 μM) in a petri dish. This was then incubated at room temperature for 1 h. After incubation, the LERCDs solution was discarded and the cells were rinsed with PBS. Then the peel was placed on the slide and observed using a fluorescence microscope.
All the results in this manuscript were represented as the mean of three individual experiments [standard deviation (±SD)].

The size and morphology of the LERCDs were studied using TEM and HRTEM as illustrated in Fig. 1A and B. The LERCDs were uniformly dispersed and spherical. Fig. 1B shows the TEM image of the LERCDs with well-resolved lattice fringes and the inset shows the HRTEM image displaying the interplanar spacing at 0.26 nm, which corresponds to the (201) lattice plane of the hexagonal structure of LaCl3 [48]. Fig. 1C shows the particle size distribution curve for the LERCDs, with an average size of 5 nm. The phase purity and structural stability of the LERCDs were investigated before and after treatment with Ca2+ using XRD analysis. Fig. 1D demonstrates the XRD patterns for LERCDs with and without Ca2+. Interestingly, sharp and distinct crystalline peaks were observed at diffraction angles of 27.4°, 31.7°, 45.4°, 56.5°, 66.2°, and 75.3°. These peaks were attributed to the lattice planes of (111), (201), (211), (131), (222), and (213), respectively. An additional peak was identified at 84.0°, which is marked with a star (*) symbol. The XRD data was consistent with the results obtained from the HRTEM and the crystallinity was also well-preserved after the formation of the LERCDs. The XRD patterns of the LERCDs did not show remarkable changes after the addition of Ca2+ ions, except decrease of the peak intensity in the Ca2+-treated LERCDs. This suggests that LERCDs form coordination with Ca2+ without effecting their crystalline structure. The elemental compositions of the LERCDs were studied using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) during scanning electron microscopy (SEM). From the elemental mapping analysis, elements including carbon (C), oxygen (O), sodium (Na), lanthanum (La), chlorine (Cl), and nitrogen (N) were detected as shown in Fig. S2. Further, Ca2+-treated LERCDs showed the calcium (Ca), which indicated the coordination of Ca2+ and the LERCDs (Fig. S3). The EDX spectra of the LERCDs and the Ca2+-treated LERCDs confirmed the presence of corresponding elements as illustrated in Fig. S4 and S5, respectively. The sodium (Na) appeared in the EDX spectra was responsible from the addition of NaOH during the reflux reaction. The thermal stability behavior of the LERCDs was studied using TGA analysis. Fig. S6 shows the thermal decomposition of the LERCDs that occur in four-stages at different temperatures. The initial weight loss was observed between 20 and 190.14 °C (4.873%) which corresponds to the loss of moisture content and surface hydroxyl (OH) groups on the LERCDs. The second and third weight loss was noticed between 190.14 and 389.28 °C (9.116%) and 389.28 and 426.81 °C (1.72%). This is attributed to the decomposition of functional groups such as an amine (−NH2), chlorine (Cl), and other oxygen-based (C=O) functional groups and other aromatic domains. The final weight loss was observed between 426.81 and 600 °C (6.451%). The total weight loss of the LERCDs after the four-stages of decomposition was found to be 22.16%, which suggests that the prepared LERCDs are highly thermally stable [49]. Again, weight loss percentages and corresponding temperature ranges for the LERCDs were listed in Table S1.

The optical properties of the LERCDs were explored in aqueous solutions using UV–Vis absorbance and PL spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the LERCDs alone (black curve) and the Ca2+-treated LERCDs (red curve) are illustrated in Fig. 2A. The LERCDs display two characteristic absorption bands at ca. 274 and 406 nm. The absorption band at 274 nm can be assigned to the π -π* transition of aromatic sp2 carbons (C=C) [18]. This aromatic group (C=C) is a carryover from the rutin precursor used to prepare the LERCDs. After the LERCDs were treated with Ca2+ this absorption band is slightly red shifted to 278 nm. The wide absorption band centered at 406 nm is assigned to the n-π* transition of the surface functional groups (C=O/C-N bonds) [43]. After binding to Ca2+, the LERCDs absorption band was shifted to 412 nm. Fig. 2B demonstrates the PL excitation spectra (curve i) (λem = 567 nm) and emission spectra (curve ii) (λex = 431 nm) for the LERCDs. The inset in Fig. 2B displays photographs of the LERCDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). The maximum fluorescence emission wavelength (λem) for the LERCDs was centered at 567 nm. Unlike other CDs directly prepared from EDTA, where emission maximum (λem = 450 nm) was noticed (i.e. blue fluorescence) [27], and EGTA- modified CDs prepared from citric acid and ethylene diamine also demonstrate emission maximum (λem = 460 nm) (blue fluorescence) [30]. We believe that the shift in the PL emission from blue to yellow can be attributed to the incorporation of the lanthanum and rutin, and the type of synthesis reaction used in this study. Fig. 2C shows the excitation-independent emission features of the LERCDs when the excitation wavelength was changed from 350 to 450 nm. This can be credited to the existence of similar kinds of surface states on the LERCDs [41]. The presence of a PL excitation wavelength around 431 nm suggests that there are C=O/C-N functional groups on the surface of these LERCDs that correspond to the yellow emission spectrum [43]. Just like other yellow-fluorescent CDs, the LERCDs also demonstrates excitation-independent emission characteristics [40–45]. Primarily, we investigated the fluorescence quenching behavior of the LERCDs using different concentrations of Ca2+ in PBS (pH: 7.4, 1× concentration). We noted strong PL quenching in the range of 10–60 μM as illustrated in Fig. 2D. Generally, PBS is being used as a suitable model for the human serum due to the similar pH value of 7.4. Hence, we believe the sensing of Ca2+ ions in PBS using LERCDs may further facilitate sensing applications in human serum [50].

The LERCDs and Ca2+ treated LERCDs (LERCDs-Ca2+) were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) to study their chemical composition. Fig. 3A demonstrates the FT-IR spectra of the LERCDs (i) and the LERCDs-Ca2+ (ii). In curve i, the LERCDs demonstrated (a) stretching vibration of COO− at 1587 cm−1 and (b) symmetric deformation of the –CH3 group (from rutin) at 1396 cm−1. This stretching band of COO− might be shifted due to the coordination between the La2+ ions and the COO− group [51]. However, in curve ii, peaks from both (a) and (b) were shifted to (blueshift) 1558 cm−1 and (redshift) 1423 cm−1, respectively. Again, this peak in curve ii (a) was more intensely sharpened compared to the peak in curve i, which corresponds to the coordination of the Ca2+ ion in this region. Remarkably, the (c), stretching vibration of the OH group at 3290 cm−1 (curve i) was red-shifted to 3351 cm−1 (curve ii). The peaks at 1020 and 1070 cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibration from CN (curve i, (d)). This suggests that tertiary amino groups from the EDTA might coordinate with La3+ [52]. Further, these peaks were blue-shifted to 1010 and 1064 cm−1, respectively (curve ii, (d)). These peak changes were shown to be directly related to Ca2+ binding. While the stretching vibration of CH at 2848 and 2917 cm−1 was red-shifted to 2918 and 2987 cm−1. The above spectral changes in FT-IR, support the hypothesis that the LERCDs form complexes with the Ca2+ ions. As the high-availability of polar functional groups including OH, CO, and CN on the surface of the LERCDs makes them readily dispersible in aqueous solutions [53]. The surface charge of the LERCDs was investigated using zeta potential (ζ) measurements. The surface charge of the LERCDs was measured as −2.49 mV. It suggested that CO groups in EDTA contributed to the negative surface charge of the LERCDs. However, after treatment with different concentrations of Ca2+ (10 and 70 μM), the zeta potential values changed to 5.82 and 7.82 mV, respectively. While the further gradual increase in the positive surface charge indicates that coordination between the LERCDs and Ca2+ exists ( Fig. 3B). In addition, the elemental composition and chemical states of the LERCDs were investigated using XPS analysis. These findings indicated that the LERCDs were predominantly composed of La 4d, Cl 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, La 3d, and Na1s. The XPS total survey spectra for these elements were shown to have peaks around 105, 198.4, 284.8, 399, 530.9, 834.9, and 1071.3 eV, respectively (curve i, Fig. 4A ). After treatment with Ca2+, all these peaks were shifted to 106, 199, 284.8, 399.3, 531.4, 835.3, 1072 eV, respectively (curve ii, Fig. 4A). Fig. 4B shows the Cl 2p high-resolution spectrum of the LERCDs around 198.4 eV, which corresponds to inorganic chlorine (metal chloride). The high-resolution spectra of C 1 s revealed two peaks at 284.8 and 288 eV assigned to C-C/C=C (sp3 and sp2) and CO groups ( Fig. 4C). These groups were observed in the precursors EDTA and rutin [54]. The high-resolution spectra of N 1 s showed a peak at 399 eV corresponding to a C-N-C group ( Fig. 4D). This chemical bonding group is also associated with the presence of EDTA in the LERCDs. This corresponds with the previously prepared ECDs through hydrothermal treatment [27]. Fig. 4E demonstrates the O 1 s high-resolution spectrum for the LERCDs at 531 eV and corresponds to CO. The presence of oxygen-containing groups like CO on the surface of the LERCDs could have a significant impact on their fluorescence. This group was shown in both the C 1 s and O 1 s high-resolution spectra [41]. From Fig. 4F, the high-resolution spectrum of Na 1 s showed a peak observed around 1071.3 eV. However, this peak was attributed to the addition of NaOH in the reaction mixture. The high-resolution spectra of La 3d showed two peaks around 836.2 and 853.1 eV, which corresponded to La 3d5/2 and La 3d3/2, respectively ( Fig. 4G). The LERCDs demonstrated characteristic peaks allocated to La 4d (105 eV), La 3d5/2 (836.2 eV) and La 3d3/2 (853.1 eV), which supports for La3+ incorporation in the LERCDs surface [34]. More importantly, two deconvoluted peaks were observed at 347 and 350.5 eV that correspond to Ca 2p3/2 and Ca 2p1/2, respectively ( Fig. 4H). This indicates the presence of Ca2+ in the LERCDs after the addition of Ca2+. The XPS spectra confirmed the results from the FT-IR analysis including the stretching vibrations for CN and COO− at 1020 and 1587 cm−1, respectively. These functional groups are responsible for the stability of the LERCDs in aqueous solutions [55]. After careful investigations of the FT-IR, Zeta potential, and XPS data we can conclude that these LERCDs can form complexes with Ca2+ ions.

The fluorescence stability of the LERCDs was investigated in different pH solutions (pH: 1–12) as illustrated in Fig. S7. The PL intensity of the LERCDs was almost stable between pH 4 and 10. LERCDs showed a gradual increase in the PL intensity in highly acidic pH conditions when compared among pH 1, 2, and 3. On the other hand, a slight decrease in the PL intensity was observed in alkaline pH of 10, 11, and 12. This trend in pH was responsible for the protonation and deprotonation of the LERCDs in acidic and alkaline pH conditions. This also corresponds with the surface functional groups (i.e. CO and CN) found on the LERCDs [56]. Remarkably, LERCDs showed high PL intensity at biological pH (c.a. pH 7). This suggests that LERCDs are the potential for sensing and bio-imaging of biologically significant elements. Moreover, the PL intensity of the LERCDs was observed up to 89% in 3 months with intervals of 10-days. The LERCDs demonstrated bright yellow color fluorescence without a serious bleaching phenomenon (Fig. S8). Also, LERCDs maintained adequate photostability under continuous illumination for 2 h using a xenon lamp, with intervals of 10-min. Fortunately, no photobleaching was observed for the LERCDs during this illumination (Fig. S9).

The detection of Ca2+ was evaluated using LERCDs in aqueous solution. Different concentrations of Ca2+ (0–70 μM) were added to the LERCDs (0.15 mg L−1) solution. The PL intensity of the LERCDs gradually decreased with increasing Ca2+ concentration as shown in Fig. 5A . In addition, the PL intensity of the LERCDs decreased by more than 53% when the Ca2+ concentration reached 70 μM. A good linear trend was observed between (F0-F/F0) over Ca2+ concentration in the linear range of 0–25 μM and the correlation coefficient was found to be R2 = 0.995 (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection (LOD) was shown to be 2.19 μM based on the equation 3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation and K is the slope of the calibration plot [57]. In general, the concentration of intracellular Ca2+ ions is around 100 nM, however, this can vary depending on the activity of various cellular functions and can reach as much as 10 μM. In addition, the extracellular cellular fluid (ECF) contains Ca2+ ions at approximately 2 mM [58]. Given these parameters, our data suggest that LERCDs have sufficient sensitivity for the detection of extracellular Ca2+ ions. Table 1 shows the comparison between the LERCDs and previously reported fluorescent CDs for the detection of Ca2+ ions.

Time-resolved fluorescence lifetime decay analysis was conducted for the LERCDs before and after treatment with Ca2+ (Fig. S10). For this purpose, the fluorescence decay curves were fitted using the double exponential function as described by the following equation:
(2)
D
t
=
∑
i = 1
n
ai
exp
−
t τi

Here D indicates the fluorescence decay, while τi and ai are fluorescence lifetime and their pre-exponential factors, respectively. Initially, LERCDs showed lifetimes of τ1 = 11.36 ns and τ2 = 0.032 ns, with both lifetimes being reduced for Ca2+-treated LERCDs with corresponding lifetimes of τ1 8.25 and τ2 0.030 ns, respectively. The big difference in lifetime values suggests that fluorescence quenching was probably attributed to non-radiative electron or energy transfer. This is in direct contradiction with some studies that have demonstrated static fluorescence quenching in the detection of Ca2+. Yue et al. reported that EGTA-modified CDs for the detection of Ca2+ which showed the fluorescence quenching via a static mechanism [30]. Kim's group have described PL quenching of EDTA-CDs for the detection of Ca2+, which was facilitated by electron transfer [27]. In the present study, we explained the fluorescence quenching of LERCDs in the presence of Ca2+ ions using electron transfer which has confirmed by time-resolved fluorescence lifetime decay studies.

On the other hand, investigations of UV–Vis absorption spectra also suggested that the coordination existed between LERCDs and Ca2+ ions, with a slight shift in the absorption band. Also, after the addition of Ca2+ ions, a significant increase in absorption takes place. To further understand this, we investigated the zeta potential analysis of the LERCDs with and without Ca2+ at two different concentrations (10 and 70 μM). A negative zeta potential value −2.49 mV was assigned to the surface charge (CO group) of the LERCDs. The LERCDs showed positive zeta potential values of 5.82 (for 10 μM [Ca2+]) and 7.82 mV (for 70 μM [Ca2+]) after the addition of Ca2+ ions. These results support the complexation between the LERCDs and Ca2+ ions. The lattice planes of the LERCDs before and after the addition of Ca2+ ions are similar as per the XRD studies. But the peak intensity has decreased slightly in the Ca2+ treated LERCDs. This implies that the LERCDs were participated in the complex formation between LERCDs and Ca2+ ions without changing their crystalline morphology.

The selectivity for the LERCDs against the Ca2+ ions has studied using different metal ions such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+, and Cr3+. Fig. 7A shows the fluorescence intensities of the LERCDs after the addition of the above ions. All the other metal ions were taken in a little higher concentration (100 μM) when compared to the Ca2+ ions (70 μM). The corresponding visual photographs for the LERCDs (i.e. blank) and the interfering ions (i-xiii) are shown in Fig. 7 B. Interestingly, iron ions (Fe3+ and Fe2+) also showed a significant fluorescence quenching for the LERCDs. The Ca2+, Fe3+, and Fe2+ ions have demonstrated the fluorescence quenching of 87.4, 73.8, and 55.2%, respectively. We believe this trend might be attributed to the presence of EDTA in the LERCDs. However, both EDTA and La3+ have played a vital role in the specificity and high selectivity of the LERCDs for Ca2+ ions when compared to the other mono, di, and trivalent ions. Further, to investigate the influence of both Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions on the LERCDs, we carried out PL experiments. For this, different concentrations (100 μM) of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions and Ca2+ (30 and 40 μM) ions were taken. No significant fluorescence quenching was observed in the LERCDs even in the presence of high concentrations of iron ions. On the contrary, LERCDs after adding low concentrations of Ca2+ ions have shown better fluorescence quenching abilities, as shown in Fig. S11. Based on these observations it suggests that as-synthesized LERCDs are demonstrating excellent selectivity among the other potential interferants such as Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions. On the other hand, the selectivity of the LERCDs was explored by recording PL spectra after the Ca2+ ions were mixed with other metal ions as illustrated in Fig. S12. It suggests that the fluorescence intensity of the LERCDs can be quenched efficiently by Ca2+ ions, though other metal ions have existed. However, the quenching efficiency of the LERCDs was not altered by the presence of other metal ions. These results have shown the high selectivity and specificity for the LERCDs in the sensing of the Ca2+ ions.

The fluorescence behavior of the LERCDs was studied using various concentrations of NaCl (0–1 mol L−1) as shown in Fig. S13. However, no significant changes were observed in the PL intensity of the LERCDs. This suggests that the LERCDs were stable in high salt concentrations. Further, this supports that the LERCDs can be employed in the sensing of biological and clinical samples.

The cytotoxicity of the LERCDs was studied in the A375 cells by the MTT assay. The cytotoxicity of the LERCDs in A375 cells was evaluated using cisplatin as a positive control as shown in Fig. S14. The cell viability was decreased by increasing the concentrations of the LERCDs. This suggests that these LERCDs may show some cytotoxic effects on cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extracellular sensing of the Ca2+ ions using A375 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The extracellular sensing of Ca2+ ions was studied using the LERCDs in A375 cells under the fluorescence microscope. Fig. 8A shows the fluorescence image of the LERCDs in A375 cells when treated with LERCDs alone. This suggests that the LERCDs have good cellular uptake in A375 cells. After the addition of Ca2+ ions at different concentrations, (10, 40, and 70 μM), to cells, the fluorescence intensity of the LERCDs in the cells was gradually decreased (Fig. 8 B, C, and D). This shows that the LERCDs have excellent extracellular sensing of Ca2+ ions towards the A375 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extracellular sensing of the Ca2+ ions using plant cells
ce:section.ce:para: The extracellular sensing of Ca2+ ions was studied by the LERCDs in onion epidermal cells using the fluorescence microscope. Fig. 9A shows the fluorescence image of plant cells after incubated with LERCDs. This suggests that the LERCDs can penetrate the cell walls. While Fig. 9 B, C, and D show that the fluorescence intensity of the LERCDs was gradually decreased after incubated with increasing concentrations of the Ca2+ (10, 40, and 70 μM), as seen in the A375 cells. This supports the sensing potential of the LERCDs in both plant studies as well as mammalian imaging.

In summary, we prepared a novel yellow color emissive LERCDs using a two-step process. First, CDs were prepared from the precursors LaCl3·xH2O and EDTA via hydrothermal treatment. Then, this solution was kept in a reflux reaction using rutin hydrate as a stabilizing agent. These LERCDs show high sensitivity and selectivity for Ca2+ ions in aqueous solutions. The LERCDs demonstrated a high quantum yield with superior photostability and excitation-independent emission. The PL quenching mechanism in the LERCDs was supported by the lifetime decay analysis. Further, surface functional group studies were analyzed by the FT-IR, XPS, and zeta potential. The LERCDs showed sufficient sensitivity and extracellular sensing in both melanoma and onion cells and emerged as a potential fluorescent probe. These LERCDs are non-toxic and have excellent biocompatibility which helps in the clinical settings.

Buddolla Anantha Lakshmi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Roopkumar Sangubotla: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jongsung Kim: Validation, Writing - review & editing. Hyung-Tae Ha: Investigation, Data curation. Sanghyo Kim: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the R&D Program from the Society of the National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT & Future Planning (2015M3A9E2031372) and by the Gachon University research fund of 2019 (GCU-2019-0385).
